title: Paper Explained: Geometry-aware similarity metrics for neural representations on Riemannian and statistical manifolds - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: Geometry Reveals How Neural Networks Differ

content.background:
Before this work, most tools for comparing neural representations looked at where points sit in a big, flat feature space and how far apart they are. It’s like judging how similar two cities are by straight-line distances on a flat map. But the data that neural networks learn to work with often lives on curved, tucked-away surfaces inside that space. If you measure with straight lines, you can miss the real shape of the space the network is using. Two networks might seem similar by these flat-distance rules, yet their internal organization—the way they bend, twist, and connect information along the curved surface—could be fundamentally different.

Why does that matter? Because those hidden differences can reflect different learning processes, different ways the network steers information through time, and different ways noise or new data will affect them. If we only compare flat distances, we might conclude two models are alike when, in fact, they solve problems in different ways or will generalize differently under real-world conditions. This makes it hard to understand what learning regimes actually do, how nonlinear dynamics play out inside networks, or how diffusion-like processes propagate representations.

In short, the motivation is to move from flat, surface-level comparisons to something that respects the true shape of the spaces neural representations inhabit. By focusing on the intrinsic geometry—the space’s own curvature and structure—we can better tease apart how different networks learn, how their internal computations differ, and how robust or adaptable their solutions might be in practice. This aims to give researchers a more faithful, interpretable lens for studying neural representations beyond what traditional, Euclidean-style comparisons could reveal.
content.methodology:
Neural networks often solve tasks in ways that aren’t obvious just by looking at the raw numbers they produce. This paper’s key innovation is a method called Metric Similarity Analysis (MSA), which uses ideas from Riemannian geometry to compare the “shape” of neural representations themselves. Instead of just comparing where points sit in a big coordinate space (extrinsic view), MSA asks: what is the intrinsic geometry of the space where the network’s representations actually live? In other words, it looks at how the representation space is curved and connected, and how distances are felt along the surface, not just across coordinates.

How they do it, conceptually (in simple steps):
- Treat neural activations as a geometric object: imagine the set of all representations the network can produce as a curved surface or manifold, shaped by the data and the network’s learning.
- Focus on intrinsic geometry: instead of Euclidean distances between points, consider how distances are measured along the surface, the local bending (curvature) of the surface, and the way small neighborhoods are arranged. This is like measuring distances along a map that can be folded without tearing.
- Build a intrinsic similarity score: compare two networks or learning regimes by looking at how their representation manifolds are shaped locally and globally. Do they bend in similar ways? Do geodesics (the shortest paths on the surface) between points align? This reveals whether the networks organize information in the same fundamental way.
- Extend to dynamics and diffusion: treat how representations evolve over time or under diffusion processes as paths on the manifold, and compare these nonlinear trajectories through the lens of the intrinsic geometry.

Why this matters and what it reveals:
- It lets researchers disentangle differences in computation that aren’t obvious from surface-level behavior. Two networks might solve the same task and look similar in raw coordinates, yet their intrinsic geometries—and thus their internal strategies—could be quite different.
- It provides a unified framework to compare nonlinear dynamics and diffusion models by examining how these processes unfold on the representation manifold.
- In practice, this geometry-aware lens helps answer questions like: are networks learning with different learning regimes in a fundamentally different way? Do diffusion-like models explore the representation space along distinct geometric routes?

In short, MSA moves the comparison from “where features sit” to “how the feature space is shaped from the inside.” By focusing on the intrinsic geometry of neural representations, it offers a mathematically grounded way to understand the mechanisms behind neural computations and how they differ across learning regimes, dynamics, and diffusion processes.
content.results:
This paper introduces a new tool called metric similarity analysis (MSA) that looks at neural representations in a deep way: it studies their intrinsic geometry. Instead of just asking how close activation patterns are in the big, flat space of all possible activations (the extrinsic view), MSA asks what the activations look like when you treat them as living on their own curved surface (the intrinsic view). This curved-surface perspective comes from Riemannian geometry and the “manifold” idea, which says high-dimensional data often lie on a lower-dimensional, curved shape. By focusing on this intrinsic shape, MSA can reveal how neural representations are really organized, beyond what simple distances can show.

Compared to older methods, which tend to compare representations by how they sit in the ambient space, MSA digs into the geometry of the surface itself—its bends, twists, and folds. This allows the researchers to disentangle different features of how networks compute things, especially when the networks have been trained under different learning setups or goals. The authors demonstrate that MSA can compare nonlinear dynamics (how representations evolve over time in a network) and can be applied to diffusion models (a class of generative models that create data by spreading information through space). In short, MSA provides a mathematically grounded, broadly applicable way to study neural computation by comparing the shapes of representations, not just their positions.

The practical impact is that scientists gain a principled, interpretable way to understand why networks solve problems the way they do. With MSA, researchers can distinguish whether two networks look similar in output but actually use very different internal strategies, or reveal how training choices shape the geometry of representations. This can guide model design, training choices, and debugging—helping us build models that generalize better, are more robust, or behave in more predictable ways. Because the method targets intrinsic geometry, it can be applied across many model types and tasks, making it a significant step toward a deeper, more universal way to interpret neural representations.
content.significance:
Think of neural representations as living on curved surfaces, not on a flat floor. Traditional similarity measures compare straight-line distances in the big activation space (extrinsic geometry), which can miss how data actually sits and moves on a curved manifold inside the network. This paper (MSA) takes the intrinsic view: it looks at the geometry that is built into the representations themselves, like distances measured along the surface of a sphere or a saddle-shaped valley. Because real neural computations often live on these curved spaces, MSA can reveal subtle differences in how networks solve problems that simple Euclidean measures miss. That matters now because modern AI systems—large language models, diffusion models, and multimodal systems—are increasingly understanding and generating complex structured information, so having a more accurate geometric lens helps us interpret, compare, and trust them.

In the long run, this work can shift how researchers study and design AI. By grounding similarity in intrinsic geometry, MSA provides a mathematically solid framework to disentangle what different learning regimes or architectures are actually doing, not just how their activations look. This could lead to new training goals or regularizers that shape the geometry of representations to improve generalization, transfer across tasks or modalities, and robustness to changes in data or prompts. It also lays a bridge between fields like Riemannian geometry, information geometry, and deep learning, enabling more principled analysis of how information diffuses and reorganizes itself inside networks. Over time, expect interpretability and model auditing tools to adopt intrinsic-geometry metrics as a standard part of understanding and validating AI systems.

Here are concrete ways this could touch systems you know. For ChatGPT-like language models, MSA can be used to study how internal token or concept representations organize on a manifold, helping researchers compare reasoning strategies across models or prompt styles without relying only on surface-level accuracy. For diffusion models and other generative systems (think Stable Diffusion or image/video editors), MSA can illuminate how latent spaces evolve and align with real data geometry during generation, potentially improving sampling quality and controllability. In multimodal and robotic AI, MSA helps compare representations across text, images, and actions, aiding transfer learning and safer, more reliable behavior. In short, this approach gives a deeper, more principled way to compare and improve AI systems, which matters as models become bigger, more interconnected, and more influential in everyday tools like chat assistants, search, and creative apps.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Riemannian geometry: The Heart of Geometry-aware similarity metrics for neural representations on Riemannian and statistical manifolds
concept_explanation.content:
Think of neural activations like people walking on the surface of the Earth. If you measure how far apart they are by drawing straight lines through space (a flat map idea), you’ll often get distances that don’t match how people would actually travel along the hills, valleys, and roads on the globe. That’s the difference between extrinsic geometry (distances in the big, ambient space) and intrinsic geometry (distances measured along the surface itself). In neural networks, the high-dimensional activations for many inputs also lie on a curved, low-dimensional surface inside the huge activation space. The paper you mentioned argues that to truly understand how networks compute, we should study that intrinsic geometry instead of just looking at straight-line distances in the big space.

So how does this work, in a simple, step-by-step way? First, assume that the network’s representations for typical inputs lie on a (roughly) smooth manifold—think of it as a bendy sheet embedded in a high-dimensional space. Next, at every point on this sheet, there is a local way to measure distances that can depend on direction; this is called a metric. In math-y terms, this local rule is encoded in a metric tensor, which tells you how far you move on the sheet in any direction. Then you look at geodesics—the shortest paths that stay on the surface itself, not cutting through the air outside the sheet. The length of these geodesics gives you intrinsic, manifold-respecting distances between representations. In practice, since we only have samples, we estimate the local geometry from nearby activations (for example, by looking at neighbors and using local linear approximations) and use them to approximate geodesic distances and related quantities. Finally, you can compare networks by comparing how their intrinsic distances organize the same set of inputs, or how diffusion-like processes unfold along their manifolds.

A concrete way to picture this is with a simple curved loop. Imagine all activations for a digit form a loop on the surface of your activation space. If you measure distances with straight lines through the space, two points on opposite sides of the loop might look far apart, even if you could travel along the loop with a short arc. But the intrinsic distance along the loop—the geodesic distance on the manifold—captures how similar those activations are when you move along the surface itself. This helps distinguish genuine differences in neural computation from mere reparametrizations or bending of the representation space. In other words, two networks might place the same digits in similar Euclidean spots, but their intrinsic geometry could reveal that they traverse different intrinsic paths to encode the same information.

Why is this important? Because intrinsic geometry is closely tied to how information actually flows and transforms inside a network. By comparing intrinsic distances and the way tangent spaces (the best flat approximation of the surface at a point) line up between networks, researchers can disentangle different learning regimes, compare nonlinear dynamics, and study how diffusion processes (like spreading information or uncertainty) behave on the learned representations. This goes beyond simple, Euclidean-style similarity and gives a more faithful, geometry-aware picture of computation. In practice, practitioners can use these ideas to diagnose how different training methods shape internal representations, compare models that solve the same task in different ways, or analyze complex generative models (like diffusion models) to see how their latent representations evolve along the manifold.

summary: This paper introduced metric similarity analysis (MSA), a Riemannian-geometry–based method that compares the intrinsic geometry of neural representations on manifolds, enabling the disentangling of neural computations, the comparison of nonlinear dynamics, and the study of diffusion models within a solid, mathematically grounded framework for understanding neural computation.
